staff.title: Staff Guide
staff.subtitle: Learn optimal hiring, training, and team management strategies for each game phase

garage-solo.title: Work Solo in the Garage
garage-solo.description: You cannot hire staff in the garage phase. Focus on building your skills and saving money for your first office.

garage-skills.title: Balance Your Skills
garage-skills.description: Train yourself in both Design and Technology. A 700/700 split is optimal for making quality games.

garage-save.title: Save for First Office
garage-save.description: You need 1 million dollars to unlock the first office. Save aggressively in years 4-5.

first-hire.title: Hire Your First Employee
first-hire.description: Hire someone with complementary skills. If you're strong in Design, hire someone strong in Technology and vice versa.

first-training.title: Train Immediately
first-training.description: New hires have low stats. Use training to boost their skills quickly. Prioritize their weakest areas first.

first-specialist.title: Specialists vs Generalists
first-specialist.description: Early game: hire generalists who can do multiple tasks. Later: specialists for specific roles in larger teams.

first-team-size.title: Team Size Tips
first-team-size.description: Small games: 1-2 people. Medium games: 2-3 people. Match team size to game size for best efficiency.

second-expand.title: Expand to 6 Employees
second-expand.description: The second office allows up to 6 employees. Gradually hire as your cash flow allows.

second-roles.title: Assign Roles
second-roles.description: Designate 2-3 employees for Design tasks (Art, Story, Sound) and 2-3 for Technology (Engine, AI, Level Design).

second-large-games.title: Large Game Teams
second-large-games.description: Large games need 4+ people. AAA games need 6 people. Ensure you have enough trained staff.

second-specialists.title: Hire Specialists
second-specialists.description: Consider Graphics Specialists, Sound Engineers, and Writers for better game quality in specific areas.

rd-lab-unlock.title: R&D Lab Benefits
rd-lab-unlock.description: The R&D lab allows you to research new engines and hardware. You need at least 4 employees to use it effectively.

rd-dedicated.title: Dedicated R&D Staff
rd-dedicated.description: Assign 1-2 employees permanently to R&D. They should have high Research speed and Technology skills.

hw-lab-unlock.title: Hardware Lab Team
hw-lab-unlock.description: Creating your own console requires a dedicated team. Assign your best Technology specialists.

hw-console-team.title: Console Development
hw-console-team.description: Building a console needs 2-3 dedicated staff for 6+ months. Plan your team capacity accordingly.

timeline.title: Timeline
timeline.subtitle: Key milestones and events throughout your Game Dev Tycoon journey

start-game.title: Game Start
start-game.description: You begin in your garage with $70K. Make small games to build experience.
start-game.action: Make Action or Adventure games - they're forgiving for beginners

pc-available.title: PC Platform Available
pc-available.description: The PC is your starting platform. No license fee required.
pc-available.action: Use PC for all your early games

govodore-64.title: Govodore 64 Released
govodore-64.description: First affordable console. Good for Casual and Action games.
govodore-64.action: Consider getting the license if making casual games

first-hit.title: Aim for First Hit
first-hit.description: By year 2, try to make a game that scores 7+ to build fans.
first-hit.action: Focus on good topic-genre combos and proper slider settings

office-goal.title: Save for Office
office-goal.description: You should have close to 1 million by now for your first office.
office-goal.action: If not, focus on medium-sized games with good combos

tes-release.title: TES Released
tes-release.description: Major console with large audience. Great for Action games.

first-office-available.title: First Office Available
first-office-available.description: With 1 million cash, you can move to your first office and hire staff.
first-office-available.action: Move as soon as you have the money

super-tes.title: Super TES Released
super-tes.action: Upgrade from TES - this is the hot platform

playsystem.title: PlaySystem Released
playsystem.description: Revolutionary 3D console. The future of gaming.
playsystem.action: ESSENTIAL: Get this license and research 3D graphics

3d-era.title: 3D Graphics Era
3d-era.description: 3D graphics become essential for high-scoring games.
3d-era.action: Research 3D Graphics v1 immediately when available

second-office-time.title: Consider Second Office
second-office-time.description: By now you should be ready to expand to a larger office.
second-office-time.action: Need 5 million cash for second office

mbox.title: mBox Released
mbox.description: New competitor in the console market.

playsystem-3.title: PlaySystem 3 Released
playsystem-3.description: HD gaming arrives. High development costs.

mbox-360.title: mBox 360 Released
mbox-360.description: Strong online features and achievement system.

own-console.title: Create Your Own Console
own-console.description: Hardware lab allows you to design your own gaming console.

genre-combo-tip.title: Master Topic-Genre Combos
genre-combo-tip.description: The right topic-genre combination is crucial for success.
genre-combo-tip.action: Use this guide to find optimal combinations

sequel-strategy.title: Sequel Strategy
sequel-strategy.description: Sequels to successful games perform better. Build franchises.
sequel-strategy.action: Wait for a 9+ score before making sequels

engine-importance.title: Custom Engine Benefits
engine-importance.description: Building custom game engines improves game quality significantly.
engine-importance.action: Invest in R&D lab for engine development

aaa-games.title: AAA Game Development
aaa-games.description: Large team + Custom Engine + Proper marketing = AAA success.
aaa-games.action: Only attempt AAA games with experienced team

checklist.subtitle: Track your progress through Game Dev Tycoon. Add milestones from the timeline or create custom goals.
checklist.addItemsHint: Add custom tasks or select milestones from below to get started.
checklist.suggestedMilestonesHint: Important events from the timeline that you might want to track:

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
faq.subtitle: Answers to the most common questions about Game Dev Tycoon. Find quick solutions to your game problems.

basics1.question: What is the best topic-genre combination?
basics1.answer: There is no universally best combination - each topic-genre combination has a fit value from -3 to +3. Use our Recommender to find the best combinations. Generally: Action fits well with Military, Sci-Fi and Fantasy. RPG works great with Fantasy and Medieval. Simulation harmonizes with Transport, City and Business.

basics2.question: How does the scoring system work in game development?
basics2.answer: Your game gets points based on: 1) Topic-Genre Fit (how well the topic matches the genre), 2) Slider settings (each genre has optimal values), 3) Platform-Genre Fit, 4) Audience-Platform Fit, 5) Technology Level, and 6) Team Skills. The sliders are particularly important - use our presets!

basics3.question: When should I move to the next office?
basics3.answer: Move when you: 1) Have enough money (at least $1M for the first office), 2) Consistently produce good games (8+ reviews), 3) Have built a stable fanbase (at least 100k fans). Moving too early is risky - the higher costs can ruin you!

sliders1.question: What do the sliders mean in game development?
sliders1.answer: The sliders distribute your development time across different areas: Engine, Gameplay, Story/Quests, Dialogues, Level Design, AI, World Design, Graphics and Sound. Each genre has optimal settings. An action game needs more Engine and Graphics, while an RPG needs more Story and Dialogues.

sliders2.question: Which sliders are most important?
sliders2.answer: It depends on the genre! Generally: Action games need high Engine and Gameplay. RPGs need high Story and Dialogues. Strategy games need high AI and Gameplay. Simulations need high World Design and Gameplay. Use our genre-specific presets for optimal results.

staff1.question: When should I hire staff?
staff1.answer: Hire staff when: 1) You're in your first office, 2) You have at least $500k, 3) You have stable income. Start with 1-2 employees and train them well. Quality is more important than quantity - one well-trained employee is better than three untrained ones.

staff2.question: Which skills should I prioritize for staff?
staff2.answer: Focus on skills that match your game strategy. For action games: Technology and Design. For RPGs: Story and Design. Generally you need at least one tech specialist and one design specialist. Train regularly - even if it's expensive.

research1.question: What should I research first?
research1.answer: Priority 1: Audiences (Young, Everyone, Mature) - massively expands your options. Priority 2: 2D Graphics V2 and better Sound. Priority 3: New genre features like Dialogues. Never research something you can't afford - always keep reserves!

research2.question: When is 3D Graphics worth it?
research2.answer: 3D Graphics is worth it from around Year 15-16 (when 3D-capable consoles appear). Before that, 2D Graphics V3/V4 is completely sufficient. Wait with the upgrade until you: 1) Have a stable team, 2) Have collected enough research points, 3) The right platforms are available.

platforms1.question: On which platforms should I develop?
platforms1.answer: Develop on platforms that: 1) Match your genre, 2) Are active (not about to retire), 3) Match your target audience. PC is almost always a safe choice. Consoles have higher license costs but also more fans. Mobile is worth it later in the game.

platforms2.question: When should I develop multi-platform?
platforms2.answer: Multi-platform is worth it when: 1) You have a larger team (4+ people), 2) The game is big enough (medium or large), 3) The platforms fit the same genre category. Never develop multi-platform in the garage - the effort is too high!

strategy1.question: How do I get better reviews?
strategy1.answer: Get better reviews through: 1) Perfect topic-genre combination, 2) Optimal slider settings (use our presets!), 3) Good technology matching the game size, 4) Not the same combination twice in a row, 5) Enough time between sequels.

strategy2.question: How do I avoid bankruptcy?
strategy2.answer: Avoid bankruptcy by: 1) Always keeping at least $50k reserve, 2) Not expanding too quickly, 3) Making small games when money is tight, 4) Taking contract work in emergencies, 5) Never investing in expensive research if you can't afford it.

strategy3.question: When should I make sequels?
strategy3.answer: Make sequels of successful games (9+ reviews), but wait at least 1-2 years between parts. Sequels sell better with established fans. Use the time in between for other projects or contract work.

advanced1.question: How does the R&D Lab work?
advanced1.answer: You unlock the R&D Lab with the third office. There you can: 1) Develop your own engines, 2) Research new technologies, 3) Develop specialized hardware (with Hardware Lab). Your own engine is the key to top games!

advanced2.question: How do I develop the best game engine?
advanced2.answer: Develop the best engine by: 1) High tech skills in the team, 2) Including all available features, 3) Investing enough time and money, 4) Regular updates. A good engine can be used for multiple games and saves time in the long run.

manual.title: Game Dev Tycoon Manual
manual.subtitle: The complete manual for Game Dev Tycoon. Learn all game mechanics, strategies, and tips.

basics.intro: In Game Dev Tycoon you start as an independent game developer in the 1980s and build your own game empire. You develop games, hire employees, research new technologies, and try to become the most successful game developer in the world.
basics.phase1Desc: You work alone and make small games. Focus on learning and first successes. Save money for moving to the first office.
basics.phase2Desc: Hire first employees. Develop medium games. Begin researching and build your fanbase.
basics.phase3Desc: Larger team, larger games. Access to R&D Lab. Develop custom engines and AAA titles.
basics.phase4Desc: Hardware Lab, custom consoles, MMOs. Dominate the market with your game empire.
basics.goal1: Develop successful games with good reviews (8+)

development.title: Understanding Game Development
development.phasesIntro: Every game goes through three phases with their own sliders:
development.phase1Desc: Here you lay the technical foundation. The sliders are:
development.combosDesc1: Not every topic fits every genre. A war game as a casual game? A children's theme as a horror game? That doesn't work well.
development.combosDesc2: The fit is rated from -3 (very bad) to +3 (perfect). Use our Recommender to find the best combinations!
development.warningDesc: If you use the same topic-genre combination twice in a row, you'll get worse reviews. Vary your games!

scoring.title: The Scoring System
scoring.intro: The quality of your game (and thus the reviews) is determined by several factors:
scoring.topicGenreDesc: How well does your topic match the genre? Fantasy + RPG = perfect. Horror + Children's theme = catastrophic.
scoring.slidersDesc: Each genre has optimal slider values. Use our presets! Wrong sliders can ruin even perfect combinations.
scoring.platformDesc: Does the platform match the genre and audience? Action games on family consoles? Not ideal.
scoring.techDesc: Is your tech up to date? Old graphics on modern consoles hurt reviews.
scoring.tip1: Use optimal topic-genre combinations (check our Recommender)
scoring.tip2: Set sliders correctly for each phase (use our Presets)
scoring.tip3: Match the platform to the genre

team.title: Team Management
team.intro: A good team is the key to success. Here's how to best manage your employees:
team.hiringTip1: Quality over quantity - one well-trained employee is better than three beginners
team.hiringTip3: Match team size to game size
team.trainingTip1: Train new employees immediately - their base stats are low
team.trainingTip3: Training is expensive but worth it
team.rolesTip1: Assign clear roles: Design (Art, Story, Sound) and Tech (Engine, AI, Level Design)
team.rolesTip2: Specialists are better than generalists for specific tasks
team.rolesTip3: Balance your team - you need both Design and Tech skills

research.title: Research & Development
research.intro: Research is essential for staying competitive:
research.priority1: Audiences (Young, Everyone, Mature) - massively expands options
research.timingTip2: Don't research what you can't afford
research.timingTip3: Save research points for important unlocks

advanced.title: Advanced Strategies
advanced.enginesDesc: Custom engines dramatically improve game quality. Invest in high tech skills and include all available features.
advanced.sequelsDesc: Make sequels of successful games (9+ reviews), but wait 1-2 years between parts. Sequels sell better with established fans.
advanced.aaaDesc: AAA games require: Large team (6+), custom engine, proper marketing. Only attempt with an experienced team.
advanced.hardwareDesc: With the Hardware Lab you can design your own console. Needs dedicated team and significant investment.

appGuide.title: How to Use This App
appGuide.subtitle: A quick introduction to all features of the Game Dev Tycoon Advisor. Find what you need quickly and optimize your gameplay.
appGuide.workflowsDesc: The most common tasks explained step by step
appGuide.featuresDesc: Click on a menu item in the sidebar to access each feature
appGuide.proTip1: Use the app alongside the game - keep it open on a second monitor or device.
appGuide.proTip2: Slider presets are the most important part - wrong sliders ruin even the best combinations.
appGuide.proTip3: Save your checklist regularly - it helps you keep track of progress.
appGuide.proTip4: For questions: Check the FAQ first, then the manual for detailed explanations.

planGameSteps: Go to 'Genre Recommender' and select your desired topic
Note the best genre for your topic
Switch to 'Platform Recommender' and select genre + audience
Open 'Slider Presets' and copy the values for your genre
Use the 'Game Planner' to combine everything
findSlidersSteps: Select your genre from the list
Read the values for all three phases
trackProgressSteps: Check off what you've already achieved
Add your own goals if desired

sources.title: Data Sources
sources.subtitle: Information about the data sources used in this application

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.subtitle: How we handle your data on this website

analytics.title: Google Analytics
analytics.description: We use Google Analytics to understand how visitors interact with our website. This helps us improve the user experience and content.
analytics.item1: Pages visited and time spent on each page
analytics.item3: Device type, browser, and operating system
analytics.item4: Referral source (how you found our website)
analytics.moreInfo: For more information about how Google processes this data, please visit:

cookies.title: Cookies
cookies.description: This website uses cookies to enhance your experience and for analytics purposes.
cookies.type1: Analytics cookies (Google Analytics) - to understand site usage
cookies.type2: Preference cookies - to remember your language and theme settings
cookies.optOut: You can disable cookies in your browser settings. Note that this may affect some website functionality.

noPersonalData.title: Personal Data
noPersonalData.description: This website does not collect personal information such as names, email addresses, or account details. We do not require registration or login. All game planning data you create is stored locally in your browser and is not transmitted to our servers.

contact.title: Contact
contact.description: If you have any questions about this privacy policy or our data practices, please contact us through the website.
